mnemonics.title: ACL vs Capability - Location
mnemonics.content: ACL = Object knows WHO (column-wise); Capability = Domain knows WHAT (row-wise). Remember: 'ACL has A for Access associated with the object, Capability has C for Carried by the domain'.

mnemonics.title: Bell-LaPadula Rules
mnemonics.content: Simple Security Property: 'No Read Up' - read at or below your level. Star Property: 'No Write Down' - write at or above your level. Remember: SS-read down, *-write up.

mnemonics.title: UNIX Permission Order
mnemonics.content: Owner's permissions come first in ls -l output (rwx), then group (r-x), then others (r--). Order: User-Group-Others or UGO.

mnemonics.title: Principle of Least Privilege
mnemonics.content: POLP: 'Provide Only Privileges Needed' - Think of it as giving keys only to doors someone needs to open, not a master key.

mnemonics.title: Protection vs Security
mnemonics.content: Protection = Internal mechanisms (HOW); Security = External policies (WHAT). Remember: Protection mechanisms enforce security policies.

acronyms.usage: Associates permissions with each resource/object (file, device)
Security model with multiple classification levels (Top Secret, Secret, etc.)
Orange Book - defines security levels (A-D) for trusted systems
Grant minimum necessary permissions to users/processes
Unique ID assigned to each user for authentication and permission checking

visualTips.visualization: Draw a table: Rows = Domains (User A, User B, Kernel), Columns = Objects (File1, File2, Printer). Cells contain rights (Read, Write, Execute). This visual shows who can access what.
For Capability: Draw a card/token held by User with list of (File, Rights) pairs. For ACL: Draw a file folder with a sticky note listing (User, Rights) who can access it.
Think of 3 permission boxes for each category: [Read] [Write] [Execute]. Checkbox marked = permission granted. Three sets (owner/group/others) give 9 checkboxes total.
Concentric circles: Inner circle (Ring 0) = Kernel, Middle rings = System services, Outer rings = User applications. Arrows show calls can only go inward (to higher privilege).

analogies.analogy: A protection domain is like a club membership. Each member (domain) has a membership card (capability) listing which rooms (resources) they can enter and what they can do there.
An ACL is like a guest list at an exclusive event. The event (object) keeps track of who (which domains) can enter and what they can do.

analogies.anal analogy: A capability list is like a VIP pass that a person carries. The pass (capabilities) lists which areas they can access and what privileges they have.

analogies.analogy: Like a hotel key card that only opens the rooms a guest has paid for, not a master key that opens all doors.
Revoking capabilities is like cancelling credit cards - hard because they're spread everywhere. Revoking ACL entries is like updating a guest list - just one place to change.
